text.title: Journey Map Components and Types
text.summary: Eight required map components and four journey types, with rules for when each type applies.

rule.statement: Every journey map must include all eight components: persona, stages, actions, touchpoints, thoughts, emotions, pain points, and opportunities.
rule.rationale: Omitting emotions produces an action log, not a journey map. Omitting opportunities produces a complaint document, not a design input. All eight components are required for the map to drive action.

list.item: Persona: who is taking the journey (specific, research-based, not a generic archetype)
Stages: major phases of the experience (Awareness, Research, Purchase, Delivery, Use, Support)
Actions: what the user does at each stage
Touchpoints: where interaction happens (web, email, app, phone, in-store)
Thoughts: what the user is thinking and asking at each stage
Emotions: how the user feels (use 1-5 scale or emotional arc diagram)
Pain points: friction and frustrations at each stage
Opportunities: specific improvement ideas tied to pain points
Current state: documents how things are today — use to find problems and build stakeholder consensus on what to fix
Future state: documents the ideal experience — use as a design target after current state problems are understood
Day-in-the-life: broader context beyond the product — use to understand competing demands on user attention
Service blueprint: includes backstage processes — use when operational failures drive user pain points

rule.statement: Label future-state maps explicitly as design proposals, not research findings.
rule.rationale: Future-state maps generated from team imagination (not research) create false consensus that a designed experience matches user needs. The distinction must be visible on the map itself.

rule.statement: Every map cell must cite a research artifact (interview ID, support ticket ID, analytics event); "no data" is a valid and required value when evidence is absent.
rule.rationale: Ungrounded cells are team assumptions masquerading as research findings. Requiring citations forces explicit identification of gaps, which then become research priorities rather than invention prompts.
